text: Copyright Law - EU, US and China This study examines and compares copyright law in the European Union (EU), the United States (US) and the People’s Republic of China (China). This study first of all identifies differences and similarities of copyright law in the three jurisdictions and after that gives several possible explanations for those differences and similarities. This study points out that, similar to many other cases in IP law, differences and similarities between the three copyright systems can be attributed to international harmonization, which is in turn rooted in the Berne Convention. A combination of factors including convergence by means of congruence and pressure and economics led in the last decade or so to China’s copyright law, even though the differences between the civilization context and legacy of these three jurisdictions are profound, rapidly increasing and large in number, and therefore overwhelming. Those differences are resulting in China’s copyright law, albeit after heavy changes and shaping of the doctrine into EU/US-image. Differences can arise from numerous sources and causes, including differences in copyright doctrine and varying underpinning objectives of copyright, which result in the acceptance or rejection of particular rights in copyright and even the presence or absence of moral rights. Another contributing element is the differing role of statute and precedent as part of the legal systems which are generally classified into the categories of common and civil law systems. As an illustrative example China in this regard is on the civil law side. However as observed above copyright underlying reasoning and also precedent has some typical common law-country characteristics, which thereby brings China in those aspects closer to the US than to the EU. But even though China is contrary to most of the other EU member states closer to the US, it nevertheless does acknowledge the existence of moral rights in accordance with the demands of the Berne Convention.
explanation: The text reads like a high-level, policy-oriented overview with polished but somewhat mechanical phrasing and a few awkward constructions, which can indicate AI generation. It could also be a concise human abstract, but the style leans toward AI-produced prose.
